findings.description: The PDF is a large-format map/figure with spatial data callouts, legends, and geographic features arranged in a complex spatial layout. The HTML converts this into a simple single-column linear document, losing the entire map-based spatial arrangement.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a geographic map with sample location callouts (SD-1 through SD-5) positioned spatially relative to roads, parks, and property boundaries. HTML renders all content as a linear top-to-bottom text document with a small embedded map image followed by sequential tables.

findings.description: The map image in the HTML is extremely small and barely legible compared to the full-page map in the PDF. The map is the primary content of this figure, and its reduction to a small thumbnail significantly impacts usability.
findings.evidence: PDF: Full-page detailed map showing roads, property boundaries, sample locations, and data callouts. HTML: Small embedded image that is difficult to read, with all the data callouts extracted as separate tables below.

findings.description: Sample data that appeared as labeled callout boxes on the map in the PDF are rendered as separate styled tables in the HTML. The data content is preserved but the visual association with map locations is lost.
findings.evidence: PDF: Data boxes (SD-1 through SD-5) with risk assessments positioned at specific map locations. HTML: Same data in sequential tables labeled by sample ID.

findings.description: The reading order changes significantly. In the PDF, the map is the central element with data callouts positioned spatially. In the HTML, content flows as: title, map image, metadata, legend, then all sample results sequentially. The spatial context that links samples to locations is lost.
findings.evidence: PDF presents all information simultaneously on a map figure. HTML linearizes: header info → small map → scale/date/project info → legend → SD-1 table → SD-2 table → SD-3 table → SD-4 table → SD-5 table → notes → references.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy has issues: h1 is 'STREAM SEDIMENT ASSESSMENT' but the document title could be 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' which is rendered as h2. Additionally, the HTML has nested <main> elements (one wrapping everything, one inside the content), which is a structural issue. The heading hierarchy skips in places with h3 elements under what should logically be subsections.
findings.evidence: HTML has h1='STREAM SEDIMENT ASSESSMENT', h2='SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL'. In the PDF, the document title block shows 'SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL' as the project name and 'STREAM SEDIMENT ASSESSMENT' as the assessment type. Both are acceptable interpretations, but having two nested <main> elements is structurally incorrect HTML.

findings.description: Risk Assessment rows are mixed into the sample data tables as regular table rows, with the 'Risk Assessment' label spanning improperly. In the PDF, Risk Assessment is a distinct sub-section beneath each sample's data. More importantly, the Risk Assessment rows use the same two-column structure but the first column contains labels like 'CR:' and 'HI:' which are not analyte names — this is structurally acceptable but the Risk Assessment header row has an empty second cell, which is a minor structural difference from the PDF's presentation.
findings.evidence: Each table includes a row 'Risk Assessment' followed by CR and HI rows. This is a reasonable conversion approach.

findings.description: The HTML has nested <main> elements — the outer <main> from the template and an inner <main> within the content body, creating a duplicate structural wrapper.
findings.evidence: HTML contains '<body><main>...<main>...</main>...</main></body>' with two nested main elements.
